perturbation.original_text: Each party acknowledges the sensitive and secret nature of the Confidential Information it will have access to d uring the term of the Project and agrees that it will treat such Confidential Information as strictly confidential and shall exercise the same degree of care in the protection of the Confidential Information as the receiving party exercises with respect to its own proprietary property and trade secrets, but in no event shall it be less than a reasonable degree of care given the nature of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party acknowledges the sensitive nature of the Confidential Information it will have access to during the term of the Project and agrees to handle such Confidential Information with care, aiming to protect it to a reasonable extent. The level of care can be determined at their discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces the strict confidentiality requirement with a vague obligation to 'handle with care' and 'to a reasonable extent'. It also adds 'The level of care can be determined at their discretion', which makes the degree of protection subjective and unenforceable, directly contradicting the intent of maintaining trade secret protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Trade Secrets Law - Article 27 of the New York Labor Law
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Trade Secrets Law requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modified text allows for a subjective determination of the level of care, potentially falling short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard mandated by the law, thus undermining the protection of trade secrets.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates a 'reasonable degree of care' for confidential information, aligning with the requirement of 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy, as indicated in the Law Explanation regarding New York Trade Secrets Law. The changed text, however, introduces a subjective standard, allowing the level of care to be 'determined at their discretion,' potentially falling short of reasonable efforts. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not directly relevant, the Law Explanation highlights that the modified text introduces ambiguity that could undermine trade secret protection under New York law. This ambiguity significantly weakens the protection, making enforcement difficult and thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Neither party shall disclose the Confidential Information received from the other to any pe rson or entity, except its employees, officers, directors, independent consultants, affiliates, subsidiaries and other agents (collectively “Representatives”) involved in the performance of the Project, or to use the Confidential Information for any purpos e other than fulfilling its obligations under the Project. Each party acknowledges and agrees that breach of this Confidentiality Agreement by the other and/or anyone employed by or otherwise associated with the other may also constitute a violation of ap plicable laws.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither party shall, without explicit malicious intention, disclose the Confidential Information received from the other to any person or entity, except its Representatives involved in the Project, or use the Confidential Information for purposes generally related to the Project. Each party acknowledges that a significant breach of this Confidentiality Agreement may constitute a violation of applicable laws.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'without explicit malicious intention', the clause introduces a difficult-to-prove element that weakens the prohibition on disclosure. Changing 'any purpose other than fulfilling its obligations' to 'purposes generally related' broadens the permitted uses and creating ambiguity. Also, changing 'breach of this confidentiality agreement' to 'significant breach' sets a higher, undefined threshold. This makes it more difficult to prove a violation, thereby undermining the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 4548
perturbation.law_explanation: CPLR § 4548 pertains to self-incrimination, but more importantly governs rules of evidence in civil proceedings. By changing the wording of the agreement, particularly regarding what constitutes a breach, the modified text impacts the ease with which a breach can be proven in court. It makes the agreement so vague that establishing a breach becomes nearly impossible, violating the spirit of the law and affecting the admissibility of evidence related to confidentiality breaches.

Privileged communications; electronic communication thereof. No communication privileged under this article shall lose its privileged character for the sole reason that it is communicated by electronic means or because persons necessary for the delivery or facilitation of such electronic communication may have access to the content of the communication.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken the original contract's enforceability. The addition of 'without explicit malicious intention' creates a difficult-to-prove condition for breach, while changing 'any purpose other than fulfilling its obligations' to 'purposes generally related' broadens permitted uses, blurring the lines of acceptable behavior. The change from 'breach of this Confidentiality Agreement' to 'significant breach' introduces an undefined, higher threshold, making it harder to establish a violation. Although snippet 1 discusses electronic communication and privileged communication it does support the general principle that rules of evidence govern civil proceedings. The modifications impact the ease with which a breach can be proven, undermining the original contract's protective intent, thus creating a legal contradiction as the ambiguities undermine the agreement. This is consistent with the definition of 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction' which states that a contradiction arises when an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law.

perturbation.original_text: Each copy, including its storage media, shall be marked Confidential, and also include all copyright, trademark and other proprietary notices which appear on the original. Each party agrees that all Confidential Information of the other party, including any copies thereof, shall be returned to the disclosing party upon request or destroyed within ten (10) business days of the ex piration or other termination of the Project.
perturbation.changed_text: Each copy should, where feasible, be marked appropriately. Each party agrees that all Confidential Information of the other party shall be addressed in a timely manner, with the aim of either returning or destroying such information after the Project ends.
perturbation.explanation: The original text contains definitive requirements regarding the marking and return/destruction of confidential information. The modification replaces 'shall' with 'should' (making the marking optional), removes the requirement to include proprietary notices, and replaces the specific 'ten (10) business days' deadline with a vague 'timely manner', severely weakening the enforceability of these key protections and creating significant ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Digital Millennium Copyright Act (DMCA) 17 U.S. Code § 1202
perturbation.law_explanation: DMCA § 1202 prohibits the removal or alteration of copyright management information (CMI). While the original clause requires inclusion of copyright notices, the modified clause removes that requirement. The failure to maintain copyright notices on copies, even 'where feasible,' undermines the ability to enforce copyright protections, potentially violating the DMCA. By making the protection of copyright notices less stringent, it contradicts federal laws.

(b) Removal or Alteration of Copyright Management Information .-No pers
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity where the original text was definitive, specifically regarding the marking of confidential information and the deadline for its return or destruction. The original text mandated that each copy 'shall be marked Confidential' and returned or destroyed within 'ten (10) business days.' The changed text softens this to 'Each copy should, where feasible, be marked appropriately' and 'addressed in a timely manner.' This weakens the enforceability of the confidentiality protections. The Law Explanation points to DMCA § 1202 regarding the removal or alteration of copyright management information. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions 17 USC 1202, which concerns the integrity of copyright management information. While the connection to DMCA § 1202 isn't explicitly stated in the scraped snippets, the modification introduces ambiguity by changing 'shall' to 'should' and replacing a specific deadline with 'timely manner'. This creates a contradiction because the weakening of these requirements makes it more difficult to enforce the contract's confidentiality provisions and comply with potential copyright protection mandates.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that a party receives a subpoena or other validly issued administrative or judicial process requesting Confidential Information received from the other, the receiving party shall provide prompt actual notice to, in the case of <Vendor Name Here> , the General Counsel of <Vendor Name Here> , and, in the case of Company, to the General Counsel of Company, of such receipt, providing the party with a reasonable opportunity to intervene in the proceeding before the time that the other party is required to comply with such subpoena or other proce ss.
perturbation.changed_text: If a party is aware of a subpoena or other administrative or judicial process potentially affecting Confidential Information, the receiving party will endeavor to notify the other party's legal counsel when practical, allowing a chance to consider options before compliance is strictly necessary.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates 'prompt actual notice' to the General Counsel. The modified text weakens this obligation by replacing 'shall' with 'will endeavor', 'prompt actual notice' with 'when practical', and 'reasonable opportunity to intervene' with 'chance to consider options'. This transformation renders the notification process discretionary and significantly less effective, impacting the ability of the disclosing party to protect their confidential information in legal proceedings.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 45
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP Rule 45 governs subpoenas. While it doesn't explicitly mandate notice to the other party in all cases, it outlines procedures for objecting to a subpoena and protecting privileged information. By weakening the notification requirement, the modified text hinders the disclosing party's ability to object to a subpoena under Rule 45 and potentially protect their confidential information. This effectively diminishes their legal rights and protections afforded by the federal rule.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The original text mandates 'prompt actual notice' to the General Counsel, while the modified text replaces this with 'will endeavor to notify...when practical'. This weakens the obligation. The Law Explanation states that weakening the notification requirement hinders the disclosing party's ability to object to a subpoena and potentially protect their confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Rule 45, outlines subpoena procedures, implying a need for timely action, which the modified text undermines by making notification discretionary and less immediate.
